title: Current Use of Traditional Contraception Methods Among Married and Sexually Active Women
description: Current use of traditional contraceptive methods among currently married women by region, with data tables for currently married and sexually active unmarried women. Source: Philippine Statistics Authority, 2022 and 2025 National Demographic and Health Survey.

definitions.any_traditional_method_pct: Any traditional method includes calendar/rhythm, withdrawal, and other traditional methods.

note: If more than one method is used, only the most effective method is considered in this tabulation. "0.0" indicates that an estimate is less than 0.05 percent. Details may not add up to total due to rounding.

insights.title: Insights on Current Use of Traditional Contraception Methods Among Married and Sexually Active Women
insights.subtitle: AI-generated insights based on National Demographic and Health Survey data on traditional contraceptive use
insights.overview: Traditional-method use among currently married women fell from 16.5 percent in 2022 to 14.1 percent in 2025, and withdrawal remained the dominant component. The decline is broad but uneven across regions and social groups, with a few places showing increases.

sections.heading: National pattern among currently married women
sections.body: At the national level, any traditional method declined by 2.4 percentage points (16.5 to 14.1). Method composition remained stable in structure: withdrawal is still the main driver (13.4 to 11.8), calendar/rhythm is smaller (3.0 to 2.3), and other traditional methods stayed near zero.

sections.heading: Regional variation remains wide, with mixed direction of change
sections.body: In 2025, regional levels among currently married women ranged from 5.1 percent in BARMM to 22.3 percent in Ilocos Region, a 17.2-point spread. High levels were also seen in Eastern Visayas (20.4) and CALABARZON (18.1). The largest declines from 2022 were in BARMM (-9.6), Bicol Region (-8.5), and Central Visayas (-7.9), while a few regions increased, including Caraga (+1.4) and Zamboanga Peninsula (+1.3).

sections.heading: Use is concentrated in midlife and among women with children
sections.body: By age, use is lower at 15-19 (8.7), rises through 35-39 (15.4), and then eases by 45-49 (12.1). By parity, women with 1-2 children are highest (16.7), while women with no children are lower (9.0). This indicates concentration around established family-building years rather than at the youngest ages or among women without children.

sections.heading: Differences from All Women
sections.body: Compared with the All Women page, the key difference is pattern shape. The age curve is much flatter at higher levels among currently married women (8.7 to 15.4) than among all women (0.6 to 12.7), where low values at younger ages pull the curve down more sharply. The parity gradient is also less extreme here: among currently married women, the gap between no children and 1-2 children is 7.7 points (9.0 vs 16.7), while in All Women it is 12.9 points (1.8 vs 14.7). Geographic variation is more polarized in this page as well, with a 17.2-point regional spread versus 8.9 points in All Women.

sections.heading: Sexually Active Unmarried Women
sections.body: Among sexually active unmarried women, traditional-method use dropped from 17.6 percent in 2022 to 11.7 percent in 2025. The decline is mainly in withdrawal (15.0 to 10.4), while calendar/rhythm also decreased (2.3 to 1.2) and other methods stayed near zero. In 2025, rural sexually active unmarried women (14.4) were higher than urban (10.3), reversing neither direction nor gap enough to offset the overall decline.

takeaways: Traditional-method use among currently married women declined from 2022 to 2025, with withdrawal still driving most of the total.
Regional differences remain large and change is uneven, with steep declines in several regions but small increases in others.
Sexually active unmarried women show a marked decline in traditional-method use, driven primarily by lower withdrawal use.
